check: A written incident response plan exists before an incident happens
description: A short document (even one page) covers who to contact, what the first steps are, and where to coordinate (a dedicated channel/call) — written and agreed on before it's needed under pressure
why_it_matters: Deciding a response process for the first time during an actual incident wastes critical early minutes/hours and increases the chance of a mistake made under pressure

check: Roles are assigned in advance (who leads, who communicates, who does technical remediation)
description: At least an incident commander role and a communications role are identified in advance, even on a small team, so there's no ambiguity about who's driving during an actual incident
why_it_matters: Without pre-assigned roles, an incident tends to either have no clear leader (slow, uncoordinated response) or multiple people independently trying to lead (conflicting actions)

check: Rotation capability for every credential type is verified before it's needed
description: The team has actually confirmed (not just assumed) that database passwords, API keys, and signing secrets can be rotated quickly, ideally rehearsed at least once
why_it_matters: Discovering during a real incident that a credential rotation process doesn't actually work, or takes hours you don't have, turns a containable incident into a prolonged one

check: Alerting exists for the security-relevant signals identified in the OWASP/monitoring checklists
description: Anomalous auth failures, unexpected access patterns, and infrastructure-level anomalies (SEC028, SEC103) actually page or notify someone, not just get written to a log nobody watches
why_it_matters: An incident that isn't detected can't be responded to — detection is the precondition for every other step in this checklist

check: A way exists for users/researchers to report a suspected security issue
description: A visible, low-friction path exists (a security@ email, a security.txt file, a bug bounty/disclosure page) for someone who finds a vulnerability to report it responsibly
why_it_matters: Without a reporting path, a well-intentioned researcher who finds a real vulnerability may either give up trying to report it or, in the worst case, disclose it publicly first because there was no better option

check: Initial severity assessed quickly using a consistent framework (e.g. the severity scale already used in this module's checklists)
description: When a suspected incident is reported or detected, an initial severity/impact assessment happens within a defined short window (e.g. under an hour for anything potentially critical), using the same Critical/High/Medium/Low framing as the rest of this module
why_it_matters: A consistent severity framework (already established across owasp-checklist.csv, api-security-checks.csv, etc.) means the team doesn't have to invent a new scale under pressure, and severity assessments are comparable across incidents

check: Containment actions are documented as they happen, not reconstructed afterward
description: Whoever is doing technical remediation notes what they changed and when (credential rotated, service isolated, traffic blocked) in real time, in the incident channel, not from memory after the fact
why_it_matters: A reconstructed-afterward timeline is unreliable and makes both the post-mortem and any required breach-disclosure reporting harder and less accurate

check: Compromised credentials are rotated immediately upon confirmed or strongly suspected compromise
description: This is the incident-response application of DO051 — rotation happens the moment compromise is confirmed or strongly suspected, not after investigation is 'complete'
why_it_matters: Delaying rotation to finish investigating first extends the window an attacker has valid credentials — rotate first, investigate the how/why in parallel or after

check: Affected systems isolated without destroying forensic evidence
description: Containment (blocking an IP, disabling an account, isolating a service) is done in a way that stops ongoing harm without wiping logs/state that would help understand what happened (e.g. don't immediately redeploy over a compromised instance before capturing its state)
why_it_matters: Acting too fast in a way that destroys evidence can prevent understanding the actual scope of a breach, which matters both for fixing the root cause and for any legal/disclosure obligations

check: Internal stakeholders informed proportionally to severity, without unnecessary delay
description: Leadership/relevant stakeholders are informed as soon as severity is reasonably understood, calibrated to actual severity — not withheld out of a desire to have 'the full picture' first, and not blasted org-wide for a low-severity, contained issue
why_it_matters: Delayed internal communication reduces the organization's ability to make informed decisions (legal, PR, resourcing) while the incident is still active, when that input often matters most

check: User/customer notification follows applicable legal requirements and honest, clear communication
description: If user data was affected, notification follows whatever legal/regulatory disclosure timeline applies (varies by jurisdiction and data type) and is written in clear, honest language about what happened and what users should do
why_it_matters: Delayed or unclear breach notification is both a potential legal liability and a significant trust/reputation cost beyond the technical impact of the incident itself

check: A single source of truth for incident status is maintained during the response
description: One document/channel is the authoritative, continuously updated status during an active incident, rather than status being scattered across DMs/threads that go stale
why_it_matters: Fragmented status information leads to duplicated effort, contradictory actions, and confusion about what's already been done versus what's still needed

check: Root cause is identified and actually fixed, not just the symptom
description: Before declaring the incident closed, the underlying vulnerability/misconfiguration that enabled it is identified and remediated — not just the immediate symptom (e.g. rotating a leaked key without also finding and fixing how it leaked)
why_it_matters: Fixing only the symptom (rotate the key) without the root cause (why was it exposed) means the same class of incident is very likely to recur

check: Systems are verified clean before being fully restored to normal operation
description: Before lifting containment measures fully, there's explicit verification (not just assumption) that the affected system is actually clean and the vulnerability is actually closed
why_it_matters: Restoring to normal operation before verification risks the same attacker (or the same vulnerability) causing a repeat incident almost immediately

check: A blameless post-mortem is conducted after every incident above a defined severity threshold
description: A structured post-incident review happens (what happened, timeline, what worked, what didn't, concrete action items with owners), explicitly framed as blameless — focused on systemic fixes, not individual fault
why_it_matters: A blame-focused post-mortem culture discourages honest reporting of near-misses and mistakes in the future, which is precisely the information needed to actually improve — blameless framing is what makes the post-mortem produce real learning instead of defensiveness

check: Action items from the post-mortem are tracked to actual completion, not just documented
description: Concrete follow-up items (fix X, add monitoring for Y, update the IR plan for Z) are tracked in the team's normal work-tracking system with an owner and, ideally, revisited to confirm they were actually done
why_it_matters: A post-mortem whose action items are never actually completed provides the appearance of learning without the substance — this is what turns 'lessons learned' into an actual reduction in future risk

check: The incident response plan itself is updated based on what the post-mortem revealed
description: If the post-mortem surfaces a gap in the IR plan itself (a role that was unclear, a rotation step that didn't work as expected), the plan (SEC108) is updated to reflect that
why_it_matters: An IR plan that's never revised based on real incident experience stays theoretical — the whole value of running a post-mortem is feeding back into making the next response better
